title: The Open Shelf Where Memory Becomes Tide

features.name: tide-marked shelf
features.desc-on-touch: a low shelf carved from stone where the archive keeps things — at high tide the sea crosses it; at low tide the marks of salt remain, a record of every water that ever read here

features.name: salt grid
features.desc-on-touch: a regular pattern of crystalline deposits marking latitude, longitude, time — each salt trace holds a memory the water is choosing to return to you before it forgets

features.name: coast-reading chamber
features.desc-on-touch: a pool that holds seawater and the memory of seawater — you dip your hand in and you know precisely when you'll forget this moment, how long the archive will hold it before the salt teaches memory to dissolve

features.name: lighthouse coordinate
features.desc-on-touch: a point of reference where grid-lines cross, marking the moment when the tidal argument decided not to choose — the light here is visible at both high and low water

features.name: memory-stone archive
features.desc-on-touch: rocks arranged by hand in patterns that mean: this was held here, this mattered, this will be returned by the next tide — the archive's smallest library, made to be flooded regularly

features.name: salt-wind glyph
features.desc-on-touch: unreadable marking in the sand that means: memory and forgetting have reached an equilibrium here, they exchange places at each tide, neither one wins

inscription: where salt keeps its grudges longer than the archive can remember, and memory learns to become tide — to return, to leave, and return again

file.name: Tidal_Salt_Deposit_Archive_Shelf_Coordinates.env
file.note: environmental record: grid coordinates of the shelf, tide tables, salinity curves — a machine-readable memory of what the water keeps returning to this place

exits.label: back to the salt cape where choosing stopped
into open water where memory becomes entirely tide
toward the estuary where river and salt negotiate their difference
